content.text: Rollback Strategies for Prompt and Config Versions

A GenAI service has more rollback surfaces than a conventional application. Beyond the container image, you can independently roll back the prompt version, the model configuration, or both — often without restarting a single pod. Understanding the rollback layers and their relative speed lets an on-call engineer choose the fastest path to recovery during an incident.

The core principle: roll back at the shallowest layer that fixes the regression. A prompt rollback takes 60 seconds and requires no image change. An image rollback takes 5–10 minutes and carries more risk. Always try prompt and config first.

attrs.alt: Rollback decision tree and artifact layers with timing for each

content.text: Layer 1: Rolling Back a Prompt Version

If prompts are stored in a Kubernetes ConfigMap and the application hot-reloads them via inotify (watching the mounted file path), a prompt rollback requires only a ConfigMap patch — no pod restart, no image change, no deployment needed.

# Fastest rollback: revert prompt in ConfigMap
# Identify the previous prompt SHA from the audit log or git log

# Option A: patch ConfigMap directly with the previous prompt content
PREVIOUS_PROMPT=$(git show HEAD~1:prompts/rag-answer/v2/system.md)
kubectl create configmap genai-prompts \
    --from-literal=rag_answer_v2_system="$PREVIOUS_PROMPT" \
    --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -

# Option B: revert git commit and re-apply the configmap
git revert HEAD --no-edit
kubectl apply -f k8s/genai-service/configmap-prompts.yaml

# Verify the running pods picked up the change (file watch)
kubectl exec -n genai-prod deploy/genai-service -- \
    sha256sum /app/prompts/rag-answer/v2/system.md

# app/prompts.py — hot-reload support via file watch
import pathlib, hashlib, threading, time, logging

log = logging.getLogger(__name__)
_cache: dict[str, tuple] = {}   # (sha, PromptVersion)
_lock = threading.Lock()

def get_prompt(feature: str, version: str):
    """Returns the current prompt, reloading if the file has changed."""
    base = pathlib.Path("prompts") / feature / version
    system_path = base / "system.md"
    current_mtime = system_path.stat().st_mtime

    cache_key = f"{feature}/{version}"
    with _lock:
        if cache_key in _cache:
            cached_mtime, prompt = _cache[cache_key]
            if cached_mtime == current_mtime:
                return prompt

    # Reload on mtime change (ConfigMap update)
    prompt = _load_prompt(feature, version)
    with _lock:
        _cache[cache_key] = (current_mtime, prompt)
    log.info("prompt_reloaded", feature=feature, version=version,
             sha=prompt.sha256[:12])
    return prompt

Hot-reload means a ConfigMap update takes effect within the polling interval (typically 60–120 seconds in Kubernetes) without any pod disruption. This is the fastest recovery path for prompt regressions.
Layer 2: Rolling Back Model Config

If the regression is caused by a model config change (wrong temperature, wrong model snapshot), revert the config file and redeploy the ConfigMap. Pods will pick up the new config on their next file-watch cycle or after a rolling restart.

# Roll back model config to the previous commit's version
git log --oneline config/model-config.prod.yaml | head -5
# a3f2c8d Update rag_answer to gpt-4o-2024-08-06
# b7d4e1f Promote rag_answer v2 config to prod
# c9e2f3a Initial prod config

# Revert to the previous version
git checkout b7d4e1f -- config/model-config.prod.yaml
git commit -m "rollback(model-config): revert rag_answer to gpt-4o-2024-05-13"
git push origin main

# Trigger configmap update (or run the deploy workflow)
kubectl create configmap genai-model-config \
    --from-file=config/model-config.prod.yaml \
    --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -

# Rolling restart to pick up new config (if no hot-reload)
kubectl rollout restart deployment/genai-service -n genai-prod
kubectl rollout status deployment/genai-service -n genai-prod
Layer 3: Rolling Back the Application Image

When neither prompt nor config rollback fixes the regression — the issue is in application code — use kubectl rollout undo to switch back to the previous image digest. Kubernetes keeps a history of previous ReplicaSets for exactly this purpose.

# View deployment rollout history
kubectl rollout history deployment/genai-service -n genai-prod
# REVISION  CHANGE-CAUSE
# 1         Initial deploy
# 2         Promote image sha:a3f2c8d
# 3         Promote image sha:b7d4e1f  ← current

# Roll back to previous revision
kubectl rollout undo deployment/genai-service -n genai-prod
# deployment.apps/genai-service rolled back

# Or roll back to a specific revision
kubectl rollout undo deployment/genai-service -n genai-prod --to-revision=2

# Watch rollout progress
kubectl rollout status deployment/genai-service -n genai-prod

# Verify the running image digest
kubectl get pods -n genai-prod -o jsonpath='{.items[0].spec.containers[0].image}'

# scripts/trigger_rollback.py
# Automated rollback script for on-call use.
# Records the rollback event to the audit log.

import subprocess, datetime, json, boto3, argparse, sys

def rollback_deployment(namespace: str, deployment: str,
                        reason: str, actor: str) -> None:
    # Execute rollback
    result = subprocess.run([
        "kubectl", "rollout", "undo",
        f"deployment/{deployment}",
        "-n", namespace,
    ], capture_output=True, text=True)

    if result.returncode != 0:
        print(f"Rollback FAILED: {result.stderr}")
        sys.exit(1)

    print(f"Rollback initiated: {result.stdout.strip()}")

    # Wait for completion
    subprocess.run([
        "kubectl", "rollout", "status",
        f"deployment/{deployment}",
        "-n", namespace,
        "--timeout=300s",
    ], check=True)

    # Audit log
    record = {
        "ts": datetime.datetime.utcnow().isoformat() + "Z",
        "event": "image_rollback",
        "deployment": deployment,
        "namespace": namespace,
        "reason": reason,
        "actor": actor,
    }
    s3 = boto3.client("s3")
    key = f"rollback-audit/{record['ts']}-{deployment}.json"
    s3.put_object(Bucket="genai-audit-log", Key=key,
                  Body=json.dumps(record, indent=2))
    print(f"Audit record written: {key}")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    p = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    p.add_argument("--deployment", default="genai-service")
    p.add_argument("--namespace", default="genai-prod")
    p.add_argument("--reason", required=True)
    p.add_argument("--actor", required=True)
    args = p.parse_args()
    rollback_deployment(args.namespace, args.deployment, args.reason, args.actor)
Rollback Runbook

Keep this runbook in your team's incident response docs. During an incident, decision speed matters — knowing the decision tree in advance prevents wasted time trying image rollbacks before the faster prompt rollback.

## GenAI Service Rollback Runbook

### Step 1: Identify the regression
- Check `/config` endpoint — confirm active model, prompt_version, prompt_sha
- Compare with the previous deploy's audit log entry in S3
- Check Datadog: filter `quality_score` metric by `prompt_sha` label to isolate which SHA degraded

### Step 2: Choose rollback layer

**If prompt_version changed in last deploy:**
  ```bash
  git checkout HEAD~1 -- prompts/<feature>/v<N>/
  kubectl apply -f k8s/genai-service/configmap-prompts.yaml
  ```
  Wait 60–120s. Re-check quality metric.

**If model config changed (temperature, model snapshot):**
  ```bash
  git checkout HEAD~1 -- config/model-config.prod.yaml
  kubectl apply -f k8s/genai-service/configmap-model-config.yaml
  kubectl rollout restart deployment/genai-service -n genai-prod
  ```
  Wait 3–5 min. Re-check quality metric.

**If neither worked (code bug):**
  ```bash
  python3 scripts/trigger_rollback.py \
      --reason "quality regression post-deploy" \
      --actor "your-username"
  ```
  Wait for kubectl rollout status to confirm. Re-check quality metric.

### Step 3: Open post-mortem ticket
- Link: rollback audit record in S3
- Link: Datadog dashboard showing quality recovery
- Action items: add eval case for the regression type
Detecting When to Roll Back: Quality Signals

A rollback is useless if you do not detect the regression in time. Instrument quality signals that fire within minutes of a bad deploy, not hours. The key metrics for GenAI services are: eval pass rate on the rolling shadow eval set, error rate on the LLM call (timeouts, refusals, format parse failures), and human feedback signals (thumbs-down rate, re-query rate).

# app/quality_monitor.py
# Runs shadow evals continuously and emits a quality_score metric.
# If quality_score drops below threshold, alert fires → on-call investigates.

import threading, time, random, logging
from prometheus_client import Gauge

log = logging.getLogger(__name__)
QUALITY_SCORE = Gauge("genai_quality_score",
                      "Rolling eval pass rate (0-1)",
                      ["feature", "prompt_sha"])

SHADOW_EVAL_CASES = [
    {"question": "What is retrieval augmented generation?",
     "context": "RAG combines retrieval with generation...",
     "expected_contains": ["retrieval", "generation"]},
    # ... more cases loaded from eval suite at startup
]

def run_shadow_eval(feature: str, service_call) -> None:
    """Runs a random subset of eval cases every 5 minutes and tracks pass rate."""
    while True:
        sample = random.sample(SHADOW_EVAL_CASES,
                               min(5, len(SHADOW_EVAL_CASES)))
        passed = 0
        prompt_sha = "unknown"

        for case in sample:
            try:
                result = service_call(**case)
                prompt_sha = result.get("prompt_sha", "unknown")
                output = result.get("answer", "")
                if all(kw.lower() in output.lower()
                       for kw in case["expected_contains"]):
                    passed += 1
            except Exception as e:
                log.warning("shadow_eval_error", error=str(e))

        pass_rate = passed / len(sample)
        QUALITY_SCORE.labels(feature=feature,
                             prompt_sha=prompt_sha).set(pass_rate)
        log.info("shadow_eval_complete", feature=feature,
                 pass_rate=pass_rate, prompt_sha=prompt_sha)
        time.sleep(300)   # run every 5 minutes

# k8s/monitoring/quality-alert.yaml (Prometheus AlertManager rule)
groups:
  - name: genai_quality
    rules:
      - alert: GenAIQualityDegraded
        expr: |
          genai_quality_score{feature="rag_answer"} < 0.85
        for: 5m
        labels:
          severity: warning
          team: platform
        annotations:
          summary: "RAG answer quality dropped below 85%"
          description: |
            Quality score is {{ $value | humanizePercentage }} for
            prompt_sha={{ $labels.prompt_sha }}.
            Check recent deploys and consider rollback.
            Runbook: https://wiki.internal/genai-rollback-runbook

      - alert: GenAIQualityCritical
        expr: |
          genai_quality_score{feature="rag_answer"} < 0.70
        for: 2m
        labels:
          severity: critical
          team: platform
        annotations:
          summary: "RAG answer quality below 70% — immediate rollback required"
Testing Rollback Procedures in Non-Production

Rollback procedures that are never exercised fail when it matters most. Run a quarterly rollback drill in the staging environment: deliberately deploy a prompt regression, detect it via the quality monitor, execute the rollback procedure, and time the recovery. Document the result.

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/rollback-drill.sh
# Quarterly rollback drill for staging environment.
# Deploys a known-bad prompt, verifies detection, then rolls back.

set -euo pipefail
NAMESPACE="genai-staging"
DEPLOY="genai-service"

echo "=== ROLLBACK DRILL START ==="
echo "Step 1: Record current quality score baseline"
BASELINE=$(curl -s http://genai-staging.internal/metrics \
    | grep 'genai_quality_score{feature="rag_answer"' \
    | awk '{print $2}')
echo "Baseline quality: $BASELINE"

echo "Step 2: Deploy known-bad prompt (empty system prompt)"
kubectl create configmap genai-prompts \
    --from-literal=rag_answer_v2_system="" \
    --dry-run=client -o yaml \
    --namespace="$NAMESPACE" | kubectl apply -f -
echo "Bad prompt deployed. Waiting 2 minutes for quality drop..."
sleep 120

echo "Step 3: Check quality degraded"
NEW_SCORE=$(curl -s http://genai-staging.internal/metrics \
    | grep 'genai_quality_score{feature="rag_answer"' \
    | awk '{print $2}')
echo "Post-deploy quality: $NEW_SCORE"

echo "Step 4: Execute rollback"
ROLLBACK_START=$(date +%s)
git checkout HEAD -- prompts/rag-answer/v2/system.md
kubectl apply -f k8s/genai-service/configmap-prompts.yaml \
    --namespace="$NAMESPACE"
echo "Rollback applied. Waiting 90s for hot-reload..."
sleep 90

ROLLBACK_END=$(date +%s)
ROLLBACK_TIME=$((ROLLBACK_END - ROLLBACK_START))

echo "Step 5: Verify recovery"
FINAL_SCORE=$(curl -s http://genai-staging.internal/metrics \
    | grep 'genai_quality_score{feature="rag_answer"' \
    | awk '{print $2}')
echo "=== DRILL RESULT ==="
echo "Baseline: $BASELINE → Degraded: $NEW_SCORE → Recovered: $FINAL_SCORE"
echo "Rollback time: ${ROLLBACK_TIME}s"
echo "=== ROLLBACK DRILL COMPLETE ==="

The drill is only valuable if you time it. If prompt rollback takes more than 5 minutes, your hot-reload is broken or your ConfigMap update pipeline is too slow — fix those before the next real incident.
Summary
GenAI services have three independent rollback layers: prompt version (60s, no pod restart), model config (2–5 min, pod reload), and application image (5–10 min, rolling restart).
Roll back at the shallowest layer first — prompt rollback is the fastest and lowest-risk recovery for quality regressions caused by prompt changes.
Implement hot-reload in the application so ConfigMap updates take effect without pod restarts; use file-mtime cache invalidation rather than TTL-based polling.
Use kubectl rollout undo for image rollbacks — Kubernetes preserves the ReplicaSet history; pin revisionHistoryLimit to at least 10 to keep enough history for deep rollbacks.
Every rollback — prompt, config, or image — must write an audit record with timestamp, layer, reason, and actor before the incident ticket is closed.
Add a new eval case for every regression that required a rollback; the scenario that caught you in production should never catch you again without a failing test.
